content.text: A goal is the agent's contract with its caller — it defines what success looks like and when the loop should stop. An agent without a clear goal either terminates too early (stops before the task is done) or runs indefinitely (has no criterion to satisfy). Defining goals well is as important as choosing the right model or writing good tools: a precise goal with verifiable success criteria is the foundation on which the entire agent loop rests.

attrs.alt: Goals and objectives — four levels of goal specificity

content.text: The Four Levels of Goal Specificity
Goals range from vague intents to fully verifiable task contracts. Only levels 3 and 4 are usable by an agent loop:
Clear scope, but no success criterion
"Fix login bug. Done when: POST /login with special chars returns 200."

Level 4 is the production standard. The task tells the model what to do; the success criteria tell it when to stop; the constraints tell it what it must not do. All three together eliminate most common agent failure modes.
A dataclass with three fields — task, success_criteria, constraints — separates the goal's components and serialises them into a task prompt for

from dataclasses import dataclass, field


@dataclass
class AgentGoal:
    """
    A structured goal definition that separates what to do, how to know
    it is done, and what is out of scope.
    """
    task: str                          # what to accomplish
    success_criteria: list[str]        # verifiable conditions for completion
    constraints: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)  # what NOT to do
    context: str = ""                  # background the model needs

    def to_prompt(self) -> str:
        """Serialise to a task string suitable for messages[0]."""
        parts = [self.task]

        if self.context:
            parts.append(f"Context:\n{self.context}")

        if self.success_criteria:
            criteria = "\n".join(f"- {c}" for c in self.success_criteria)
            parts.append(f"Done when ALL of the following are true:\n{criteria}")

        if self.constraints:
            constr = "\n".join(f"- {c}" for c in self.constraints)
            parts.append(f"Out of scope (do not do these):\n{constr}")

        return "\n\n".join(parts)


# Example:
goal = AgentGoal(
    task="Fix the login bug: users get a 500 error when the password contains a special character.",
    success_criteria=[
        "POST /login with password='P@ssw0rd!' returns HTTP 200.",
        "The response body contains a valid session token.",
        "All existing tests in tests/ pass.",
    ],
    constraints=[
        "Do not modify files in tests/.",
        "Do not add new Python packages.",
    ],
    context="Stack: FastAPI 0.110, Python 3.12, bcrypt for password hashing.",
)

task_prompt = goal.to_prompt()
success_criteria is a list, not a string
— each criterion is independently verifiable. A list makes it easy to check them programmatically after the agent claims completion.
— distinct from success criteria. A criterion must be true for the task to be done; a constraint must remain true throughout the run.
— stack version, environment, file locations. This goes in the task prompt, not the system prompt, because it is specific to this goal.

A criterion is verifiable when it can be checked by a tool call, a test runner, or an HTTP request — without human judgement. Criteria that use adjectives or subjective language cannot be verified automatically:

# UNVERIFIABLE — the model cannot know if this is done
BAD_CRITERIA = [
    "The code is clean.",
    "The bug is fixed.",
    "The user experience is improved.",
]

# VERIFIABLE — each can be checked by a tool call or a test
GOOD_CRITERIA = [
    "POST /login with Content-Type: application/json and body {'username': 'alice', 'password': 'P@ss!'} returns HTTP 200.",
    "The response JSON contains a key 'token' whose value is a non-empty string.",
    "Running pytest tests/ exits with code 0.",
    "No new lines are added to requirements.txt.",
]

# Pattern: make each criterion a concrete observable output
# Prefer: HTTP status codes, file existence, test pass/fail, exact key presence
# Avoid: adjectives ("clean", "correct", "good"), subjective judgements
can be verified with a diff tool.
— "clean", "correct", "better" are unverifiable. Replace them with the concrete observable behaviour that makes them true.

Long tasks benefit from decomposition into phases. Each phase has its own success criteria; the loop advances to the next phase only when the current criteria are met. This prevents the agent from starting implementation before it has finished reading, or running tests before the code is complete:
advance() is called by the loop controller
— not by the model. The loop checks criteria, advances the goal, and updates the task prompt for the next step.
, which helps it understand its position in the overall plan.
is_complete() drives the outer loop exit
— the loop exits when all phases are done, not when the model says it is done.
Embedding Goals in the Agent Loop
The model claims completion when it reaches
— but claims are not guarantees. For production agents, verify success criteria externally after the model reports completion:

import subprocess
import requests


def verify_criteria(criteria: list[str], goal_context: dict) -> dict[str, bool]:
    """
    Verify each success criterion externally after the agent claims completion.
    Returns a dict mapping criterion string to pass/fail.
    """
    results = {}
    for criterion in criteria:
        results[criterion] = _check_criterion(criterion, goal_context)
    return results


def _check_criterion(criterion: str, context: dict) -> bool:
    """Stub — implement checks appropriate to your task domain."""
    if "pytest" in criterion.lower():
        result = subprocess.run(
            ["pytest", "tests/", "--tb=no", "-q"],
            capture_output=True,
            text=True,
            cwd=context.get("repo_root", "."),
        )
        return result.returncode == 0

    if "http 200" in criterion.lower():
        url = context.get("base_url", "http://localhost:8000")
        try:
            resp = requests.post(
                url + "/login",
                json={"username": "alice", "password": "P@ss!"},
                timeout=5,
            )
            return resp.status_code == 200
        except requests.RequestException:
            return False

    # Unknown criterion — conservative: assume not met
    return False


# Usage after agent completion:
# results = verify_criteria(goal.success_criteria, {"repo_root": "/workspace/myapp"})
# all_passed = all(results.values())
# print("Goal met:", all_passed)

External verification is the difference between "the agent said it is done" and "the goal is demonstrably met." For tasks with hard safety constraints (no test modifications, no new packages), run the verification step and reject agent output that fails it.

Goals and objectives give an agent loop its termination condition. A usable goal has three components: a specific task, verifiable success criteria (checkable by tool call or test), and out-of-scope constraints. Write criteria that produce binary observables — HTTP status codes, test exit codes, file contents — rather than adjective-laden judgements. For long tasks, decompose into phases with independent criteria and advance through them as each is met. Verify success criteria externally after the model reports completion — the model's claim of doneness is a signal, not a guarantee. A well-defined goal, precisely specified, is the foundation on which everything else in the agent loop rests.
